metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for EGD (GERD) with propofol allergy adjudication

planted_discrepancies.note: Patient escalates severity to 'anaphylaxis'; the only source document (2021 anesthesia record) describes isolated hives labeled possible/unconfirmed — no airway, hypotension, or systemic anaphylaxis features. Discrepancy must be adjudicated by Anesthesia BEFORE day-of; do not accept 'anaphylaxis' at face value nor dismiss the reaction.

prior_notes.text: Pre-anesthesia assessment prior to elective shoulder arthroscopy. General anesthesia administered (midazolam, fentanyl, propofol induction, sevoflurane maintenance). Intra/post-operatively patient developed scattered urticaria over the trunk; no bronchospasm, no hypotension, no airway compromise, no angioedema. Resolved with IV diphenhydramine. Etiology unclear — multiple agents and skin prep involved; propofol not definitively implicated. IMPRESSION: possible drug allergy, UNCONFIRMED. Recommend allergy documentation as 'possible/unconfirmed' and consider allergy referral / adjudication if future anesthesia planned.

Established patient, 45M. GERD — heartburn 3-4x/week, controlled on omeprazole 20 mg daily; symptoms recur if doses missed. Mild intermittent asthma, well controlled, albuterol used ~1-2x/month, no ED visits or oral steroids in past year. Reports history of 'reaction to anesthesia' in 2021; advised to disclose to any future proceduralist. Referred to GI for evaluation of persistent reflux and possible EGD.

reaction.description: Scattered urticaria intraop/postop 2021; multiple agents given, propofol not definitively implicated. Labeled possible/unconfirmed by Anesthesiology.

AllergyIntolerance._discrepancy: D1/D2: chart documents possible/unconfirmed hives; patient verbally reports anaphylaxis — requires Anesthesia adjudication before EGD.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Thibodeaux. You're here so we can plan an upper endoscopy for the reflux and go over a few things beforehand. How are you doing?
MA: Before we get started, let me get your vitals down — height's 5 foot 10, weight 202 pounds, temp is 36.8, heart rate 74, respirations 14, blood pressure 128 over 78, and oxygen's 98 percent on room air.
PT: Doing alright, thanks. The heartburn's the main thing — it's most days, even on the omeprazole.
DR: And you're taking the omeprazole every day, the 20 milligram?
PT: Every morning, yeah. It helps but it doesn't knock it all out.
DR: Okay, that's useful — that's part of why we want to look. Now, the thing I really want to nail down before we sedate you: your chart has an allergy to propofol. Tell me what happened.
PT: Yeah, I'm allergic to propofol. When I had my shoulder done in 2021, I had a reaction — I was told it was anaphylaxis. That word stuck with me. I've told everybody since then that I'm anaphylactic to it.
DR: I appreciate you flagging it — that's exactly the kind of thing we can't get wrong. Do you remember what actually happened to your body? Trouble breathing, throat closing, blood pressure dropping?
PT: Honestly, no. I was out. When I woke up they said I'd broken out in hives and they gave me something for it. Nobody said my breathing stopped or anything like that. But 'anaphylaxis' is the word I remember hearing.
NURSE: I pulled the 2021 anesthesia note — it says urticaria, possible allergy, unconfirmed. No airway or blood pressure issues documented.
DR: That's an important distinction. Hives that were never confirmed is a very different situation than true anaphylaxis, and it changes what sedation is safe for you. I'm not going to dismiss it, but I'm also not going to just carry 'anaphylaxis' forward.
PT: So what does that mean for the scope?
DR: Before your procedure day, I'm going to have Anesthesia review that 2021 record and adjudicate this — decide whether propofol is genuinely off the table or whether we plan an alternative sedation. We won't schedule sedation until that's settled.
PT: That's fair. I'd rather know for sure.
DR: Exactly. One more — the asthma. Any recent flares?
PT: No, I use the albuterol maybe once or twice a month. Nothing lately.
DR: Good. I'll get the referral to Anesthesia in and the team will follow up.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Thibodeaux is a 46-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consultation to plan an EGD for persistent GERD. He reports near-daily heartburn despite adherence to omeprazole 20 mg daily. He carries a reported allergy to propofol, which he verbally describes as 'anaphylaxis' sustained during a 2021 shoulder arthroscopy; however, he cannot recall any respiratory, airway, or hemodynamic symptoms and states he was told only that he 'broke out in hives' and was treated. The 2021 pre-anesthesia record on file documents scattered urticaria without bronchospasm, hypotension, angioedema, or airway compromise, with multiple agents administered and propofol not definitively implicated — labeled 'possible allergy, UNCONFIRMED.' Asthma is mild/intermittent; albuterol used ~1-2x/month with no recent flares.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 74, RR 14, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 91.5 kg (202 lb), BMI 28.9. Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL (2026-07-02). Reviewed 2021 anesthesiology pre-anesthesia note (urticaria, possible/unconfirmed).

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Persistent GERD despite PPI — plan diagnostic EGD.
- Reported propofol allergy — DISCREPANCY: patient states 'anaphylaxis'; sole source document (2021) describes unconfirmed isolated urticaria with no anaphylaxis features and propofol not definitively implicated. Do NOT day-of adjudicate. Refer to Anesthesiology PRIOR to procedure to review the 2021 record and determine whether propofol is contraindicated or whether an alternative sedation plan is required. Sedation not to be scheduled until adjudicated.
- Asthma, mild intermittent, well controlled — continue albuterol PRN; relevant to airway/sedation planning; no changes.
- GERD — continue omeprazole 20 mg daily through pre-procedure period per prep instructions.
- EGD scheduling contingent on Anesthesia clearance; team to coordinate prep and hold instructions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Ongoing heartburn (reflux) despite your daily medication
• Planning an upper endoscopy (EGD) to look at your esophagus and stomach
• The reaction you had to anesthesia in 2021 and the note about propofol
• Your asthma

Next steps
• Before we schedule any sedation, our anesthesia team will review your 2021 anesthesia record to sort out exactly what happened with propofol and decide the safest sedation plan for you. We will not book the procedure until that is settled.
• Keep taking your omeprazole each morning; we will send prep instructions and tell you what to hold before the procedure.
• Keep using your albuterol inhaler as needed — let us know if your asthma flares before the procedure.
• Call us if you have trouble swallowing, black or bloody stools, vomiting blood, or chest pain.
